records.ety: [F., from L. sententia, for sentientia, from sentire to discern by the senses and the mind, to feel, to think. See Sense, n., and cf. Sentiensi.]

quotes: Tales of best sentence and most solace.
The discourse itself, voluble enough, and full of sentence.

defs: An opinion; a decision; a determination; a judgment, especially one of an unfavorable nature.
A philosophical or theological opinion; a dogma; as, Summary of the Sentences; Book of the Sentences.
quotes: My sentence is for open war.
That by them [Luther's works] we may pass sentence upon his doctrines.

defs: In civil and admiralty law, the judgment of a court pronounced in a cause; in criminal and ecclesiastical courts, a judgment passed on a criminal by a court or judge; condemnation pronounced by a judicial tribunal; doom. In common law, the term is exclusively used to denote the judgment in criminal cases.
quotes: Received the sentence of the law.

defs: A short saying, usually containing moral instruction; a maxim; an axiom; a saw.
A combination of words which is complete as expressing a thought, and in writing is marked at the close by a period, or full point. See Proposition, 4.
quotes: He fills, he bounds, connects, and equals all.
notes: ☞ Sentences are simple or compound. A simple sentence consists of one subject and one finite verb; as, “The Lord reigns.” A compound sentence contains two or more subjects and finite verbs, as in this verse: -

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sentenced (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Sentencing (?).]

defs: To pass or pronounce judgment upon; to doom; to condemn to punishment; to prescribe the punishment of.
quotes: Nature herself is sentenced in your doom.

defs: To decree or announce as a sentence.
